summary: Joel Spolsky argues that writing software is fundamentally a design activity, not manufacturing or even craftsmanship in the traditional sense. He illustrates this through a real example of file import code in CityDesk that went from a simple three-step process to a complex multi-process architecture just to handle the rare case of very large files gracefully. Drawing a parallel to physical construction where misaligned screws are too expensive to fix, he defines software craftsmanship as the willingness to spend 500% more effort to handle the 1% edge cases correctly. He concludes that this level of polish is only economically viable for mass-market software, where the cost can be spread across enough users to justify it.
key_points: Software development is design, not manufacturing — the 'software factory' concept from the 1980s was misguided and never worked

Handling edge cases properly often requires orders of magnitude more code and complexity than the happy path, as illustrated by a simple file import becoming a multi-process architecture with progress bars
True craftsmanship means getting rare cases exactly right, even when it takes 500% more effort to fix a 1% defect
Physical construction suffers the same problem — misaligned screws that are millimeters off would require replacing entire doors to fix
Craftsmanship is expensive and only economically justified for mass-market software where costs are spread across many users
money_quotes: Making software is not a manufacturing process.
Writing code is not production, it's not always craftsmanship (though it can be), it's design.
Design is that nebulous area where you can add value faster than you add cost.
This turned out to be a great example of one of those places where 'the last 1% of the code takes 90% of the time.'
Years of experience have shown me that programming with multiple threads creates much additional complexity and introduces whole new categories of dangerously frightful heisenbugs.
The moral of the story is sometimes fixing a 1% defect takes 500% effort.
When software is built by a true craftsman, all the screws line up.
More effort went into getting rare cases exactly right than getting the main code working.
key_insight: True software craftsmanship means investing disproportionate effort into handling rare edge cases gracefully, because that's what separates delightful software from merely functional software.
